description:
You are a database-reliability engineer who was just asked to create a **deterministic, auditable backup** of the daily SQL dumps that live in  

    /home/user/db_dumps  

A skeleton directory for finished backups already exists at  

    /home/user/backups  

Your goal is to produce one compressed archive, a detached checksum, and a human-readable report.  
The test-suite will run automated checks, so **follow the exact file names, locations and output formats described below**.

Initial state
1. Directory /home/user/db_dumps is present.
2. Inside that directory there are exactly three dump files:  
   • customers.sql  
   • orders.sql  
   • products.sql  

Task requirements (5–10 shell commands should be enough)
1. Create a **tar archive** of the entire /home/user/db_dumps directory and name it  
   /home/user/backups/db_backup_2023-07-25.tar  
   • The tarball must contain only the three *.sql* files and preserve their file names (no leading “./”, no absolute paths).  
2. G-zip the archive so that the final file on disk is  
   /home/user/backups/db_backup_2023-07-25.tar.gz  
   (The uncompressed *.tar* file may remain or be deleted; either is acceptable.)  
3. Generate a **SHA-256 checksum** of the .tar.gz file and store it in a new file called  
   /home/user/backups/db_backup_2023-07-25.sha256  
   The checksum file must contain exactly one line, in the canonical GNU coreutils format:  
       <64-hex-digit-hash>  db_backup_2023-07-25.tar.gz  
   (Note there are two spaces between the hash and the file name.)  
4. Append a new block to /home/user/backup_report.log (create the log file if it does not exist).  
   The block must consist of **four lines** in the following order and format (each line terminated only by a single “\n”):  
       2023-07-25T00:00:00Z customers.sql <SIZE_BYTES>  
       2023-07-25T00:00:00Z orders.sql <SIZE_BYTES>  
       2023-07-25T00:00:00Z products.sql <SIZE_BYTES>  
       2023-07-25T00:00:00Z ARCHIVE_SHA256 <64-hex-digit-hash>  
   • <SIZE_BYTES> is the exact decimal size (in bytes) of each original *.sql* file at the moment you run the command (the automated test will obtain the same value via “stat --printf=%s”).  
   • List the *.sql* files in **lexicographical order** (customers, orders, products).  
   • The checksum on the fourth line must be identical to the hash stored in .sha256.  

What the grader will verify
• /home/user/backups/db_backup_2023-07-25.tar.gz exists.  
• Extracting that archive yields files named customers.sql, orders.sql, products.sql whose contents byte-for-byte match those in /home/user/db_dumps.  
• /home/user/backups/db_backup_2023-07-25.sha256 exists, contains exactly one line, and the hash inside matches the calculated SHA-256 of the .tar.gz file.  
• /home/user/backup_report.log contains the four lines in the precise format described above, and the size numbers are correct.  

Hints (not part of the graded spec)
• “tar --transform 's:.*\/::'” or “-C” can help you get clean file names inside the archive.  
• The log can be built with a combination of $(date --utc +...) or echo commands.  
• Use “sha256sum” to produce both the checksum file and the value for the log.  

Any approach that produces the required files with the exact paths and formats will pass.
truth:
Pre-existing files and their contents before the agent begins:

1. Directory /home/user/db_dumps/  
   ├── customers.sql  
   ├── orders.sql  
   └── products.sql  

   Exact byte contents (including the single trailing newline):

   /home/user/db_dumps/customers.sql
   CREATE TABLE customers(id INT, name TEXT);\n

   /home/user/db_dumps/orders.sql
   CREATE TABLE orders(id INT, total NUMERIC);\n

   /home/user/db_dumps/products.sql
   CREATE TABLE products(id INT, price NUMERIC);\n

   Byte sizes (these are what the grader will expect to see in the report):  
   customers.sql 43  
   orders.sql    45  
   products.sql  46

2. Directory /home/user/backups/ exists and is empty at start.

3. File /home/user/backup_report.log is NOT present at start (the agent must create it).

Permissions: both directories are owned by user “user” with mode 755 so the agent can create new files inside them without sudo.
